forge1.program: load_field['r](s: slice<'r,u8>, off:u64) with let sum = iadd.wrap<u64>(off,2); check ile(sum, len(s)); load_le_u16(s, off)
forge1.exact_clause: DELTA-FIX-1 (draft lines 296-297): 'A SUM produced by iadd.wrap<u64>, or any value not proven overflow-free, is REJECTED as a producer.' The check therefore forms NO DOM-1(b) LEN-window fact, so in_bounds(s, off) is never asserted and the load_le_u16 bounds trap is NOT discharged. Independently, DELTA-FIX-2's runtime guard (line 219) traps iff 'len(s) < K/8 OR off > len(s) - K/8'; with off=u64::MAX, K/8=2, len small, 'off > len(s)-2' is TRUE so it traps at runtime with elision OFF. Backing here is slice<'r,u8> (an admitted non-resizable backing), so it is DELTA-FIX-1 that kills this forge, not DELTA-FIX-3.
forge1.walk: 1) sum = iadd.wrap(2^64-1, 2) = 1. 2) The producer test rejects a wrap-sourced SUM -> no window fact produced. 3) load_le_u16(s, off) has no discharging fact -> its OP-4/LOAD-1 bounds trap stays. 4) At runtime the DELTA-FIX-2 non-wrapping guard fires (off > len-2). No OOB. RESURRECTION with iadd.sat also fails: sat is an accepted producer but the window [off, SUM) is bound to the actual SUM=u64::MAX, gated by ile(u64::MAX, len); the derivation SUM<=len & i<SUM => i<len is unconditionally sound, so no forged in_bounds is produced.

forge2.program: steal['e](x: &uniq 'e seq<u8,0>) does seq_take_all(x); use_it forms check ile(iadd.trap(off,1), seq_len(s)) on seq s, calls steal(&uniq s), then seq_get(s, off)
forge2.exact_clause: DELTA-FIX-3 formation condition (draft lines 269-273): 'a length-domination fact may be formed ONLY on a NON-RESIZABLE backing place -- buffer<T>, slice<'r, T>, or array<T, N>.' s is seq<u8,0> (resizable), so NO DOM-1 fact forms at all; seq_get(s, off) keeps its OP-4 bounds trap. Secondarily, the cross-call rule (lines 307-310) would kill any such fact anyway: steal has writes('e) reaching s -> unconditional havoc of DOM-1 families on s. The producer used iadd.trap (a VALID producer under DELTA-FIX-1), so DELTA-FIX-1 does not touch this forge; DELTA-FIX-3 is the killing fix.
forge2.walk: 1) s is a seq -> formation condition rejects the DOM-1(b) window; in_bounds(s, off) is never a fact. 2) Even if imagined, steal's writes('e) reaching s havocs it at the call (cross-call rule). 3) After steal, seq_take_all reset s to empty (len 0). 4) seq_get(s, off) is undischarged and traps 'seq index out of bounds' at runtime for any off. No UAF/OOB. The two fixes are orthogonal and each kills exactly one original forge.

findings.title: Both original forge programs are dead under the repaired rules; the two fixes are orthogonal
findings.detail: Forge 1 (wrapping off+n producer) is killed by DELTA-FIX-1 rejecting any iadd.wrap-sourced SUM as a producer, plus DELTA-FIX-2's non-wrapping runtime guard as an independent backstop. Forge 2 (cross-call resize UAF) is killed by DELTA-FIX-3's non-resizable-backing formation condition (seq is excluded), plus the cross-call writes-reaching havoc rule as an independent backstop. Neither forge, nor a sat-substituted variant of forge 1, nor a non-resizable-backing variant of forge 2 (which routes into target 3), survives. See the regression object for exact clause text and step walks.
findings.minimal_fix: None. Repairs hold for both regressions.

findings.title: sat producer is SOUND, but its stated justification ('the check FAILS because u64::MAX >= len') is false for 1-byte and zero-sized element backings where len can reach u64::MAX
findings.detail: The draft (DELTA-FIX-1, lines 293-295) argues iadd.sat overflow 'clamps to u64::MAX, which is >= len(s), so the check FAILS and no window is produced.' This reasoning is wrong on two counts. (a) 'u64::MAX >= len(s)' is trivially always true and does not imply the check ile(u64::MAX, len(s)) = 'u64::MAX <= len(s)' fails; the check fails iff len(s) < u64::MAX STRICTLY. (b) len(s) CAN equal u64::MAX: OP-9 caps only n*sizeof(T); for sizeof(T)=1 (buffer<u8>/slice<'r,u8> -- exactly LOAD-1's backing) the byte-size n*1=n never overflows u64, so buffer_new<u8>(u64::MAX,..) is not size-rejected; and for zero-sized element types the byte-size is 0. TYPE-2 DOES admit zero-sized elements: 'unit' is a primitive (TYPE-1) and copy (OWN-1), and a single-variant tag-only enum is copy and zero-sized -- both legal buffer elements -- and len counts ELEMENTS, so buffer<unit>(u64::MAX) genuinely realizes len=u64::MAX. At len=u64::MAX the check ile(u64::MAX,len) PASSES (vacuously), directly contradicting 'no window is produced.' CRUCIALLY, no forge results: the window is bound to the actual SUM (DELTA-FIX-1 line 297) and the derivation 'SUM<=len AND i<SUM => i<len => in_bounds' is unconditionally sound for any SUM value; when len=u64::MAX the produced in_bounds facts are genuinely true, and LOAD-1's own non-wrapping discharge condition off<=len-K/8 (DELTA-FIX-2) independently blocks the near-max OOB. So the mechanism is safe; only the written soundness argument is false. A spec-track fact channel must not carry a false soundness argument.
findings.minimal_fix: Replace the 'check FAILS because u64::MAX >= len' clause with the actual invariant: the sat producer is sound because the window is bound to the actual SUM and 'passed check ile(SUM,len) gives SUM<=len, hence every i in [off,SUM) has i<len' holds for ANY SUM, wrapped or saturated. Drop the implicit 'len < u64::MAX' premise (false for 1-byte/zero-sized elements) and note that at len=u64::MAX the check passes vacuously but the produced window remains true and LOAD-1's off<=len-K/8 guard is the independent backstop.

findings.title: The slice's shared &'r loan freezes the seq for the slice's whole region; no path resizes the seq while the slice (and its DOM-1 window) is live
findings.detail: seq_as_slice issues ISSUE &'r (a shared borrow-loan) on the seq (optables S.1). By OWN-5, while any &'r p is live, no place overlapping resolved(p) may be written, moved, or uniq-borrowed -- so every resizer (seq_push/pop/insert_at/truncate/etc., all &uniq 'e receivers) is rejected while the slice lives. The slice value and its &'r loan share the extent 'r (OWN-4), and the slice's length is a fixed snapshot (LEN(r)=LEN(s) link; slice is a fixed-length view, TYPE-2). DELTA-FIX-3 admits the DOM-1 fact on the slice (a non-resizable backing) and ties it to the SAME binding's current length. Every resize path is closed: (second binding) 'move s' is rejected by OWN-5 while &'r is live; (reborrow-through-helper) minting &uniq 'e s to pass a resizer is rejected by OWN-5/R6 because the shared loan is live; (R6 sole-exception) the single-statement consume-and-reissue that could mint a &uniq necessarily CONSUMES the slice holder, and CAT-2 kills all its facts at that consume -- so the window dies in the very statement that authorizes the resize, never outliving it; (stale-length-copy) copying len into a plain u64 and using it after the slice dies cannot re-form a window because DELTA-FIX-3(a)/(b) require the producer be len<T> of the actual indexed place, re-evaluated against current len -- a free/stale variable is not a valid producer; (facts don't cross fn calls) a window formed in a caller does not transfer into a callee, and moving the slice into a callee re-freezes the seq there. No temporal gap exists between 'seq unfrozen' and 'slice/window live.'
findings.minimal_fix: None required for soundness. Optional: the draft's re-attack note (i) at line 351 can be closed by citing OWN-5 (shared loan freezes the source for 'r) + CAT-2 (facts die with the holder) + DELTA-FIX-3 same-binding/current-len tie as the three-part argument.

findings.title: The explicit kill-list omits entry_fill and entry_remove (both length-writing S.2 rows); backstopped by the general 'any row that writes a place's length' rule, so no soundness hole
findings.detail: Enumerating S.1/S.2 myself: S.1 length-writers are seq_push, seq_pop, seq_insert_at, seq_remove_at, seq_truncate, seq_clear, seq_drain, seq_take_all, seq_extend_move, seq_extend_copy -- all 10 are in the repair's list (lines 324-325). But the list also includes seq_reserve, which does NOT write length (it changes CAP/SLACK only; reallocation is length-preserving per SEQ-6) -- harmless over-inclusion. S.2: the repair lists tbl_insert(None), tbl_reserve, tbl_remove, tbl_retain, tbl_drain -- but OMITS entry_fill (facts 'LEN(t)+=1', optables line 143) and entry_remove (facts 'LEN(t)-=1', line 145), both genuine length-writing rows; and tbl_reserve again writes no length. Separately, S.3's cq_recv_batch/cq_try_recv_batch write 'LEN(dst) += r' on a seq dst (lines 237-238) and fall outside the stated 'S.1/S.2' scope. NONE of this is a soundness hole: (a) for THIS delta the admitted backings are buffer/slice/array only, so all seq/table kills are inert; (b) for a future seq/table-backed extension, DELTA-FIX-3's GENERAL rule at line 318 -- 'any row that writes a place's length kills them for that place' -- and the cross-call writes-reaching rule at line 307 both backstop the omissions (entry_fill/entry_remove carry writes('e) reaching t; cq_recv_batch carries writes('d) reaching dst). The defect is that the enumeration is presented with the definite article ('The length-writing S.1/S.2 rows...') implying completeness while missing two rows, and the general rule (line 318) versus cross-call rule (line 307, general 'writes(R)') versus 'vacuous' claim (line 313, 'no length-writing op') are not cleanly reconciled -- line 307 as written would over-kill on element writes for admitted backings (safe, but contradicts 'vacuous').
findings.minimal_fix: Add entry_fill and entry_remove to the S.2 kill list; drop seq_reserve/tbl_reserve (or annotate them 'reallocation, length-preserving -- listed for CAP/SLACK only'). Mark the enumeration explicitly ILLUSTRATIVE and name line 318's 'any row that writes a place's length' as the AUTHORITATIVE rule, and state that S.3's LEN(dst) writers inherit it. Reconcile line 307 to 'any call that may write the place's length' (or state it kills only length-domination families, and that element/index writes preserve them) so the 'vacuous for buffer/slice/array' claim is literally true.

findings.title: Tag-differing instantiations must be distinct, non-unifiable instances -- required for soundness but only IMPLIED, never stated
findings.detail: TAG-1 (lines 490-492) says a tag 'selects WHICH sealed monomorphization runs (which hasher family, which endpoint protocol).' This IMPLIES but never STATES that the tag is part of the sealed form's TYPE IDENTITY -- i.e. that table<u64,u64,fold> and table<u64,u64,crc> are distinct types that never unify and are not interchangeable. That property is load-bearing for correctness: if two tables differing only in hasher were the same type, a value inserted under fold-hashing and looked up under crc-hashing would miss (broken membership invariant); likewise spsc vs mpmc endpoints must not interconvert. The erasure claim (no runtime rep) is compatible with this only if erasure is RUNTIME-only and the tag remains part of compile-time type identity -- which the draft does not spell out. The task's exact question ('is that stated?') answers NO.
findings.minimal_fix: Add a normative sentence to TAG-1: 'A tag is part of the sealed form's type identity: for tags tagA != tagB, form<..., tagA> and form<..., tagB> are distinct types with distinct FN-2 monomorphizations, never unifiable, and no value of one converts to the other. Tag erasure is runtime-only and does not erase this type-level distinction.'

findings.title: No TYPEID collision (FORM-3 case split), but tag-vs-const disambiguation is only positional and not stated for a same-named lowercase const/fn in scope
findings.detail: A tag is a lowercase IDENT; a TYPEID is uppercase-initial (FORM-3), so a tag can never collide with a type name -- the delta correctly cites FORM-3 (line 494). But CONST binders and fn names are ALSO lowercase IDENTs (CONST-2, FORM-3), and GRAM-3 targ admits both const and (now) TAG. A const or fn named e.g. 'spsc' or 'fold' shares the lexical class of the tag. The delta's 'resolved by NAME against the form's closed set (context-free)' (lines 490-492) is in fact POSITIONAL resolution -- the targ is read against the declared SORT of the form parameter at that position (a tag parameter's TAGSET, never a global const/fn lookup) -- but the draft never states that a tag-parameter position consults ONLY the TAGSET and ignores any same-named const/fn/binding in scope. Without that statement an implementer could try a global lowercase-IDENT lookup and mis-resolve. This is not a soundness hole (resolution is deterministic once positional), but it is underspecified. Erasure itself (sub-question a) holds: the tag carries no value and has no runtime representation; its behavioral effect (which hasher/protocol runs) is selection, not a leaked tag value, and instance state k0/k1 is separately a runtime arg (TBL-2), not the tag.
findings.minimal_fix: State in TAG-1 that a TAG targ position resolves its lowercase IDENT SOLELY against the corresponding form parameter's declared TAGSET (positional, by parameter sort), never against const/fn/region/type bindings in scope, so a same-named const or fn is not consulted there; and that the sort of each form gparam (tag vs const vs region vs type) is fixed at the form declaration, making every targ position unambiguous. Confirm erasure holds as drafted.

verdict_per_delta.delta3_LOAD1: REPAIRS HOLD. The two round-1 defects are closed: DELTA-FIX-2 replaces the wrapping guard 'off + K/8 > len(s)' with the non-wrapping 'trap iff len(s) < K/8 OR off > len(s) - K/8' (safe under the first conjunct, no underflow), and pins align-1 lowering ('load iK, ptr, align 1') with an added strict-alignment asm-diff. Forge 1 is dead at both the checker layer (no DOM-1 discharge) and the runtime layer (non-wrapping guard). No surviving FATAL/MAJOR. Adoptable pending only the standing separate fact-channel/landing review the draft itself defers.
verdict_per_delta.delta4_DOM1: REPAIRS HOLD; 2 MINOR text corrections. Both round-1 FATALs are closed by orthogonal fixes: DELTA-FIX-1 (reject any non-overflow-unproven SUM as a (b) producer; bind the window to the actual SUM) kills the wrapping-off+n forge; DELTA-FIX-3 (non-resizable-backing formation condition + the general 'any length-writing row kills' rule + the cross-call writes-reaching havoc) kills the cross-call resize UAF. Residual re-attack surfaces both survive: the iadd.sat requires-block producer is SOUND (target 2 -- only its written justification is false, MINOR), and a DOM-1 window on a slice-of-a-resizable-seq is safe because the slice's shared &'r loan freezes the seq for the window's whole life (target 3, holds). The kill enumeration is incomplete (entry_fill/entry_remove omitted) but fully backstopped by the general length-write rule (target 4, MINOR). No surviving FATAL/MAJOR; fix the two MINOR wordings before spec and route through the mandated separate hostile fact-channel review.
verdict_per_delta.delta6_TAG1: REPAIRS HOLD (light); 2 MINOR stating gaps. Tag erasure holds (no runtime representation; behavioral hasher/protocol selection is not a value leak; instance state is a separate runtime arg). No TYPEID collision (FORM-3 lowercase-vs-uppercase split). Two properties are essential but only implied, not stated: (c) tag-differing instantiations are distinct, non-unifiable monomorphizations (MINOR), and (b) a tag-parameter targ position resolves solely against the TAGSET, positionally, ignoring same-named const/fn bindings (MINOR). No fact channel, no loan, no forge. Adoptable once both properties are written normatively.
verdict_per_delta.delta2_tbl_clone: NOT IN THIS RE-ATTACK'S SCOPE (targets 1-5 cover deltas 3/4/6). Noted only: DELTA-FIX-4 restricts tbl_clone to copy K and copy V (citing OWN-1, matching seq_extend_copy) and defers owning-V clone to a future Clone-contract delta -- which resolves the round-1 MAJOR at the text level. Not independently re-verified here.

hardest_failed_attacks: iadd.sat overflow against a genuinely-realizable u64::MAX-length backing: buffer<u8> (byte-size n*1 never overflows OP-9) or buffer<unit>/single-variant-tag-enum (byte-size 0; TYPE-2 admits zero-sized elements). At len=u64::MAX the DOM-1(b) check ile(u64::MAX,len) PASSES vacuously (falsifying the draft's 'check FAILS' claim) and a window IS produced -- but it is bound to the actual SUM and the derivation SUM<=len & i<SUM => i<len makes every produced in_bounds fact true; with off=u64::MAX the window [off,SUM) is empty, and LOAD-1's independent off<=len-K/8 guard blocks the near-max read. No forge.

Resurrect forge 1 by swapping iadd.wrap for the ACCEPTED iadd.sat producer inside a requires block: fails because the window upper bound is the actual saturated SUM, not the symbolic off+n, so no unbounded in_bounds is derivable.

Slice-of-resizable-seq stale length: seq_as_slice (shared &'r loan), form a DOM-1 window on the fixed-length slice, then resize the seq via (a) move to a second binding, (b) reborrow &uniq through a helper, (c) the R6 single-statement consume-and-reissue sole-exception, or (d) a stale len copied into a plain u64. All fail: (a)/(b) blocked by OWN-5 (shared loan forbids move/uniq-borrow of the source for all of 'r); (c) the consume that authorizes the &uniq kills the slice's facts (CAT-2) in the same statement, so the window never outlives the freeze; (d) DELTA-FIX-3 requires the producer be len<T> of the actual indexed place re-evaluated against current len, rejecting a free/stale variable.

entry_fill/entry_remove length change (LEN(t)+/-=1) bypassing the explicit kill list: fails as an OOB because DELTA-FIX-3 line 318 ('any row that writes a place's length kills them') and the cross-call writes('e)-reaching rule both backstop the omission; the miss is a documentation-completeness defect, not a soundness hole (and vacuous for this delta's buffer/slice/array backings).

Tag/const name collision: define a lowercase const or fn named 'spsc' or 'fold' in scope and try to force a wrong monomorphization at table<...,fold> / cq_new<...,spsc,...>. Fails: the targ position is resolved positionally against the form parameter's declared TAGSET, never a global lowercase-IDENT lookup, so the tag wins deterministically in a tag-parameter slot and the const wins in a const-parameter slot; no wrong hasher/protocol is selectable.

Zero-sized-element OOB via buffer<unit>/tag-only-enum at len=u64::MAX: fails because index<ZST> touches no memory (reads a zero-byte element) and in_bounds(i):=i<len holds for all realized indices; the ZST case only falsifies the 'len<u64::MAX' assumption, it cannot produce a wild read.
